coredata.dc:title: Bi4O5Br2 nanosheets modified by carbon quantum dots: Efficient BPA degradation induced by enhanced absorption and Z-scheme charge transfer
coredata.dc:creator: Zang, Jiyuan
Chen, Changzhao
Chen, Xinxin
Yang, Yu
coredata.dc:description: Constructing semiconductor photocatalysts with enhanced absorption and rapid carrier dynamics is essential for applying semiconductor photocatalysis in environmental remediation and energy conversion. In this study, we modified carbon quantum dots (CQDs) onto the surface of Bi4O5Br2 using a one-step hydrothermal method. The modification enabled the excellent degradation of bisphenol A (BPA) due to the up-conversion luminescence of CQDs and the Z-scheme charge transfer path formed by the unique energy level of the two materials. The CQDs/Bi4O5Br2 composite added with 4 mg of CQDs completely degraded BPA (20 mg/L) within 100 min when the radiation wavelength exceeded 400 nm, and Bi4O5Br2 itself only degraded BPA by 1.44%. In sharp contrast, if a xenon lamp was directly irradiated without a 400 nm filter to sift the ultraviolet components, the degradation rate of the Bi4O5Br2 monomer could reach 40% under the same conditions. This indicates that introducing CQDs plays a vital role in visible-light-driven degradation. Furthermore, on the basis of the structural characterisation, the successful introduction of CQDs was confirmed in the CQDs/Bi4O5Br2 hybrid system. On this basis, the possibility of Z-scheme charge transfer was proved by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and ultraviolet electron spectroscopy measurements. The degradation mechanism of BPA under Z-type charge transfer was proposed through electron paramagnetic resonance, free radical capture and total organic carbon tests. This work is expected to provide a new idea for designing an energy band that matches semiconductor heterojunction photocatalysts, enhancing the unique narrow band gap structure and up-conversion properties of CQDs. In addition, it facilitates the construction of photocatalysts with better performance.

head.ce:title: Bi4O5Br2 nanosheets modified by carbon quantum dots: Efficient BPA degradation induced by enhanced absorption and Z-scheme charge transfer

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanics and Optoelectronics Physics, Anhui University of Science and Technology, Huainan 232001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Mechanics and Optoelectronics Physics, Anhui University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechanics and Optoelectronics Physics, Anhui University of Science and Technology, Huainan 232001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Constructing semiconductor photocatalysts with enhanced absorption and rapid carrier dynamics is essential for applying semiconductor photocatalysis in environmental remediation and energy conversion. In this study, we modified carbon quantum dots (CQDs) onto the surface of Bi4O5Br2 using a one-step hydrothermal method. The modification enabled the excellent degradation of bisphenol A (BPA) due to the up-conversion luminescence of CQDs and the Z-scheme charge transfer path formed by the unique energy level of the two materials. The CQDs/Bi4O5Br2 composite added with 4 mg of CQDs completely degraded BPA (20 mg/L) within 100 min when the radiation wavelength exceeded 400 nm, and Bi4O5Br2 itself only degraded BPA by 1.44%. In sharp contrast, if a xenon lamp was directly irradiated without a 400 nm filter to sift the ultraviolet components, the degradation rate of the Bi4O5Br2 monomer could reach 40% under the same conditions. This indicates that introducing CQDs plays a vital role in visible-light-driven degradation. Furthermore, on the basis of the structural characterisation, the successful introduction of CQDs was confirmed in the CQDs/Bi4O5Br2 hybrid system. On this basis, the possibility of Z-scheme charge transfer was proved by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and ultraviolet electron spectroscopy measurements. The degradation mechanism of BPA under Z-type charge transfer was proposed through electron paramagnetic resonance, free radical capture and total organic carbon tests. This work is expected to provide a new idea for designing an energy band that matches semiconductor heterojunction photocatalysts, enhancing the unique narrow band gap structure and up-conversion properties of CQDs. In addition, it facilitates the construction of photocatalysts with better performance.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As an efficient, safe and environment-friendly technology, semiconductor-based photocatalysis technology shows potential application value in many fields, such as organic pollution degradation, hydrogen production from splitting water, CO2 reduction and heavy metal-ion treatment [1–5]. Owing to the direct use of sunlight, this technology is the ideal candidate to solve energy and environmental problems that perplex the world today [6–8]. In terms of the degradation of organic pollutants, the two most concerning visible light-driven semiconductor catalysts, namely, g-C3N4 and MoS2, as well as their derivatives and composite structures, have been widely studied in the degradation of conventional dyes such as methyl orange (MO), methylene blue (MB) and rhodamine B (RhB). They have achieved excellent performance, even reaching the level of industrial application [9–11]. In addition to the organic dye molecules represented by MO, MB and RhB introduced because of the discharge of industrial wastewater from the printing, dyeing and textile industries, bisphenol A (BPA) threat caused by the extensive use of plastic products exists in the human living environment. It is far more harmful than the above-coloured dye molecules in terms of the breadth and depth of the impact. It is reported that on average, the world produces approximately 27 million tonnes of plastic containing BPA annually [10]. BPA can disrupt the endocrine in humans and can be highly harmful to the health of newborns. Obesity caused by cancer and metabolic disorders is also thought to be related. Therefore, it is necessary to find a suitable semiconductor catalyst that can degrade BPA efficiently and conveniently because traditional catalysts based on g-C3N4 and MoS2 are not satisfactory in this respect.

Bismuth oxide semiconductors (Bi2O3, BiOX (X = Cl, Br, I), Bi2WO6, etc.) are often used to degrade BPA [12,13] effectively because of their unique electronic structure. Hybridisation of Bi 6 s and O 2p orbitals can enlarge the valence band (VB) boundary and can increase VB divergence, which contributes to the separation of electron–hole pairs during photocatalysis and affects the catalytic activity of bismuth compounds in visible light [14]. Recently, bismuth bromide oxide (BiOBr) has shown great potential to address environmental issues as a class of anisotropic sandwich-structured photocatalysts with modulated absorption of sunlight, allowing the viable reduction of photoexcited electrons. This material achieves an interlaced layer structure with [Bi2O2]2+ and double Br− plates, which constructs an internal electric field and promotes the separation of charge carriers [15]. Conversely, Bi4O5Br2 is formed by increasing the O and Br atoms in the intercalation layer. As a result, it has a more negative VB position and can generate more superoxide ions through hydrolysis. However, the nearly 2.5 eV band gap of Bi4O5Br2 limits visible light absorption, and a single Bi4O5Br2 photocatalyst also has a high photogenerated carrier recombination rate [13]. Therefore, it is imperative to develop a highly active visible photocatalyst based on Bi4O5Br2 through modification or compounding. In this respect, some nanocomposites based on Bi4O5Br2, such as Bi4O5Br2/Bi2S3 [16], Bi4O5Br2/g-C3N4 [17], Bi4O5Br2/Ti3C2 [18], g-C3N4/ZnO/Bi4O5Br2 [19] and FeVO4/Bi4O5Br2/BiOBr [20], have been successfully synthesised to broaden and enhance photocatalysis application. However, improving photocatalysis performance is unsatisfactory because of their limited ability to use the solar spectrum.

Loading carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on Bi4O5Br2 nanosheets is an effective way to simultaneously increase light absorption and accelerate carrier separation [21,22]. CQDs are usually composed of amorphous and crystalline carbon nuclei, which contain different oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl. As a new class of carbon nanomaterials with a size less than 10 nm, CQDs can be used as electronic mediators, spectral converters, adsorption centres, photosensitisers and single photocatalysts because of their excellent solar energy spectral utilisation, adjustable energy band structure, green environment and low cost [23–26]. Many reports have shown that CQDs are synthesised with other semiconductor materials as electron reservoirs and photoinduced electron transporters. Therefore, they can significantly enhance carrier separation efficiency and visible light utilisation in guided photocatalysts, promoting charge carrier migration and separation within semiconductors, such as CQDs/TiO2 [27], CQDs/g-C3N4 [28], MoS2/CQDs/ZnIn2S4 [29] and CQDs/BiWO3 [30]. Bi4O5Br2, with a unique electronic structure, is more catalytic when combined with CQDs. On the one hand, the band gap of CQDs is approximately 0.6 eV, which has good absorption of ultraviolet, visible and even near-infrared light [31]. In particular, its unique up-conversion photoluminescence (PL) characteristics can convert long-wavelength light into short-wavelength ultraviolet light, broadening the application range of the spectrum. Bi4O5Br2 modified by CQDs also has photocatalytic activity even under light with a wavelength greater than 500 nm (approximately 2.5 eV). On the other hand, the tunable band gap structure of CQDs can be used to construct a Z-scheme heterojunction with Bi4O5Br2 because the VB edge of CQDs is close to the conduction band edge of Bi4O5Br2, which facilitates the separation of electron–hole pairs [32,33].

On the basis of the complementary advantages of CQDs and Bi4O5Br2, we prepared CQDs/Bi4O5Br2 heterostructures using a hydrothermal method that overcomes the shortcomings of low solar utilisation and high photogenerated carrier recombination rate of a single Bi4O5Br2. Although there are several reports regarding Bi4O5Br2 modification by CQDs [15,34,35], the critical difference from the current system is that either the early synthesis method of CQDs or CQDs/Bi4O5Br2 is too simple and inefficient, or application fields of photocatalysis are different. For example, He et al. [35] prepared a CQDs/Bi4O5Br2 hybrid by mixing CQDs and Bi4O5Br2 through simple physical stirring for killing lung cancer (A549) cells in vitro, wherein the precursor for preparing CQDs is glucose and ethylenediamine solution. This simple physical mixing often produces a loose heterostructure with weak interfacial coupling, affecting charge carriers’ transfer efficiency [36]. Wang et al. reported the construction of carbonised polymer dots/Bi4O5Br2 composites using the oil bath stirring method for CO2 reduction. Bi4O5Br2 was only prepared by mixing Bi(NO3)3•5 H2O, hexamethylenetetramine and KBr physically. The current work is based on the full hydrothermal route to synthesise CQDs, Bi4O5Br2 and CQDs/Bi4O5Br2 composites. Citric acid is used as the precursor for the synthesis of CQDs. On this basis, CQDs/Bi4O5Br2 is synthesised, and BPA degradation investigation using catalysts is conducted. The degradation ability of the Bi4O5Br2 monomer for BPA is found to be sensitive to the radiation wavelength. The degradation efficiency was 40% in 100 min under direct irradiation of a xenon lamp but only 1.44% under visible light irradiation. In sharp contrast, the CQDs/Bi4O5Br2 composite catalyst shows robust BPA degradation to visible light, which can be completely degraded in the same interval. In addition, Bi4O5Br2 dotted with CQDs strongly degrades antibiotic residues such as tetracycline (TC), completely decomposed after exposure to visible light for approximately 18 min. This demonstrates the composite’s universality in degrading organic species. On the basis of the ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS), electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR), total organic carbon (TOC) content and free radical capture measurements, we clarified the degradation mechanism. In addition, we proved that superoxide ion is the main active species for BPA degradation.

All reagents were purchased from Ara Industrial Ding Co. without further purification. The main drugs and chemical reagents include citric acid monohydrate, ethylenediamine, polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP), branched polyethyleneimine (PEI), bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi (NO3)3•5 H2O), potassium bromide (KBr), bisphenol A (BPA) and tetracycline (TC).

CQDs were prepared according to the synthesis path proposed by Liu et al. [37]. 0.96 mg of citric acid was stirred thoroughly with 10 ml of deionized (DI) water in a beaker until the citric acid was completely dissolved. The 335 μL of ethylenediamine was dissolved in it and stirring was continued to mix the solution completely. The mixed solution was placed into a 40 ml Teflon autoclave and heated hydrothermally at 200 °C for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained red solution was dialyzed in a 1-liter beaker for 24 h and the deionized water was changed every 8 h. Finally, the CQDs solution was obtained and further freeze-dried for 24 h to obtain about 15 mg of CQDs solid.

KBr (2 mmol), 0.5 g of PVP and a certain amount of PEI were dissolved into DI water (50 ml) to form a homogenous solution under ultrasonication. To make a white suspension, 2 mmol Bi(NO3)3•5 H2O powder was dissolved in 20 ml of water and vigorously stirred for 1 h. During this period, the prepared solution was rapidly added to the above white suspension and mixed thoroughly. Then, in an 80 ml Teflon-lined autoclave, 180 °C hydrothermal heat was employed for 3 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was washed several times alternately with DI water and ethanol, collected and dried in a 60 °C oven for 24 h. Finally, the yellow powder was obtained by grinding.

A specific mass of CQDs was dissolved in the above Bi4O5Br2 precursor solution. CQDs/Bi4O5Br2 composites in different proportions were prepared by hydrothermal heating at 180 °C for 3 h in an 80 ml reactor. The CQDs amounts were 3, 4, 5, 7 and 9 mg, and the corresponding compounds were abbreviated as CBiOBr3, CBiOBr4, CBiOBr5, CBiOBr7 and CBiOBr9.

To characterize the crystal structure and determine the phases, an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Rigaku) equipped with Cu- Kα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm) was used. A Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer was used to measure X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) for analyzing the chemical composition, interfacial bonding and valence state of catalysts. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra with the samples dispersed in KBr were obtained on a Nicolet 6700 Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer to determine the functional group species in the catalyst and its chemical environment. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were observed by a JEM-2100HR transmission electron microscope at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV for the observation of topography and lattice fringes. Ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–vis) spectra were measured by a Shimadzu UV2600 UV–vis spectrometer with BaSO4 powder as a reference. Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra were measured by an FSL-980 spectrofluorometer. UPS is used to obtain secondary electron cutoff energy using He I radiation (21.22 eV). Two reactive oxygen species, superoxide radicals (
• O
2
−
) and hydroxyl radicals (•OH), were detected using 5,5-dimethyl-1-dimethyl-N-oxide (DMPO) in combination with the EPR (MS-5000) technique. The DMPO-
• O
2
−
and DMPO-•OH concentrations were measured in methanol and aqueous solutions, respectively. An amplitude parameter of 10 mV, a frequency response range of 0.1–100 kHz, and an RHE of 0.55 V were selected for electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements in a dark and confined environment. The Mott-Schottky (MS) plots were recorded at frequencies of 1 kHz, 5 kHz and 10 kHz and amplitudes of 10 mV. The TOC content was analyzed by the Shimadzu TOC-L CSH total organic carbon (TOC) analyzer to calculate the degree of mineralization.

The photocatalytic activity was evaluated by testing the degradation of different samples against BPA and TC solutions under the irradiation of a xenon lamp (300 W). Photocatalytic experiments were performed in a specific machine in which 20 mg of photocatalyst was dispersed into 20 mg/L of a colorless BPA solution/TC solution. The suspension containing BPA contaminant and photocatalyst was sonicated in the dark for 10 min and maintained with stirring for 1 h to reach adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Under two illumination conditions with one direct irradiation of a xenon lamp and the other of loading a 400 nm filter on the xenon lamp to filter ultraviolet light (the light source was 20 cm away from the solution while stirring at a constant speed), 4 ml of the suspension was removed at fixed intervals and then filtered by centrifugation to eliminate the sample powder at each fixed interval. The concentration of the remaining BPA/TC solution was recorded on a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UVmini-1280, Shimadzu).

To detect the active species in the photocatalytic process of TC, potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7) (5 ml), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) (5 ml), benzoquinone (BQ) (5 mg) and isopropyl alcohol (IPA) (5 ml) were introduced in the TC degradation experiments to quench electron (e-), hole (h+), superoxide radical (
• O
2
−
) and hydroxyl radical (•OH), respectively. The degree of mineralization was calculated by analyzing the TOC content through the Shimadzu TOC-L CSH Total Organic Carbon (TOC) analyzer following the equation:

Fig. 1(a) shows the XRD pattern of the synthesised CQDs. A noticeable peak at 25.9° belongs to CQDs (020), the typical characteristic peak of amorphous carbon materials [38]. Fig. 1(b) shows the XRD plots of the Bi4O5Br2 monomer and CQDs/Bi4O5Br2 composite with different amounts of CQDs. The prepared Bi4O5Br2 can be well indexed to the standard card (ICSD No. 94498), indicating that the obtained Bi4O5Br2 has a monoclinic crystal structure [13]. The distinct peaks at 29.9°, 32.1° and 45.7° correspond to the Bi4O5Br2 (11−3)/(003), (402)/(020) and (422) diffraction peaks, respectively. Introducing CQDs causes the diffraction peak of Bi4O5Br2 to move slightly. Still, it does not introduce additional impurity phases, indicating that CQDs and Bi4O5Br2 are well combined with no chemical reaction. With the gradual increase in CQDs, the (002) characteristic peak of CQDs/Bi4O5Br2 at 26.3° is enhanced, proving the successful introduction of CQDs. However, as the amount of CQDs increases, the weak peak of Bi4O5Br2 in the XRD plot of CQDs/Bi4O5Br2 increases, which could be due to the excess CQDs affecting the synthesis of Bi4O5Br2 during the synthesis process, hence changing the crystal structure of the Bi4O5Br2 monomer itself and decreasing photocatalytic performance. The subtle changes in the Bi4O5Br2 (402)/(020) diffraction peak after adding CQDs are depicted in the upper right corner of Fig. 1(b). According to Bragg’s law of
2 d
sin
θ = k λ
[39], moving the diffraction angle to a high angle indicates that the crystal plane spacing decreases. It indicates that the electronic coupling and stress mismatch at the interface affect the Bi4O5Br2 lattice.

FT-IR and Raman tests were performed on various fabricated samples to further demonstrate the successful introduction of CQDs, as shown in Fig. 1(c) and (d). As shown in Fig. 1(c), the peak at 538 cm−1 correlates with the Bi–O stretching mode, which appears in pure Bi4O5Br2 and CQDs/Bi4O5Br2 hybrids. Furthermore, the peaks at 1770, 1646 and 1501 cm−1 are associated with –CO–, –CN and –N–H of CQDs, respectively, which confirms the presence of CQDs in the Bi4O5Br2 composite [34].

A Raman test was performed to determine the surface structure differences between the prepared Bi4O5Br2 monomers and CQDs/Bi4O5Br2 materials. The peaks at 65.6 and 114.5 cm−1 (Fig. 1(d)) are assigned to the A
1 g internal Bi–Br stretching modes of Bi4O5Br2. The other peak centred at 160.0 cm−1 can be attributed to the Bi–Br stretching mode of E
g
[13]. After introducing CQDs, the peak intensities of A 1 g and E g decreased drastically. In particular, the peak at 114.5 cm−1 shifted significantly to 105.9 cm−1. In addition, the Raman spectrum shape of the CBiOBr4 composite is close to that of CQDs, indicating that introducing CQDs greatly inhibited Bi–Br vibration.

The morphological characteristics and elemental distribution of the CQDs/Bi4O5Br2 composite were further analysed using TEM, HRTEM and elemental mapping. TEM images of CBiOBr4 are shown in Fig. 2(a), where CQDs of a diameter of approximately 10 nm are attached to the flaky Bi4O5Br2. Two distinct crystal planes with spaces of 0.195 nm (Fig. 2(c)) and 0.279 nm (Fig. 2(d)) can be distinguished from the HRTEM image of the CBiOBr4 composite (Fig. 2(b)), corresponding to (100) of CQDs and (020) of Bi4O5Br2 [40,41]. The fast Fourier transform spectra of the two lattices are shown in Fig. 2(d) and (f). The separated diffraction spots show that CQDs and Bi4O5Br2 have good crystallisation quality. The mapping test of the CBiOBr4 composite shows (Fig. 2(g)–(k)) that all five elements of Bi, O, Br, C and N are present and evenly distributed in the CQDs/Bi4O5Br2 composite, indicating that the CQDs/Bi4O5Br2 heterojunction was successfully synthesised.

Fig. S1 shows the XPS tests on Bi4O5Br2 and CBiOBr4. As shown in Fig. S1(a), only Bi, O, Br, C and N are in the samples. On the basis of the calibration of the sp3 hybrid C–C bond derived from surface amorphous carbon at 284.6 eV (Fig. S1(b)), we analysed the high-resolution XPS spectra of C, N and Bi in Bi4O5Br2 and CBiOBr4 materials to reveal the effect of introducing CQDs on the binding energy of elements. The core energy level spectra of C 1 s (Fig. S1(b)) and Bi 4 f (Fig. S1(d)) did not change when CQDs were added. Furthermore, the N 1 s signal (Fig. S1(c)) is almost negligible in pure Bi4O5Br2, which contrasts sharply with the rich N elements in CBiOBr4 composites. This is because the N elements in CBiOBr4 composites mainly come from the hydroxyl or carboxyl groups formed on the surface of CQDs [42,43]. We further calculated each element’s XPS peak area and relative ratio in the Bi4O5Br2 monomer and CBiOBr4 (Table S1). We can see that the ratios of C 1 s and N 1 s in CBiOBr4 are 5.5% and 1.8%, respectively, significantly higher than the ratios of C 1 s and N 1 s in the Bi4O5Br2 monomer (4.6% and 1.1%, respectively). The results further prove that CQDs successfully formed a heterojunction with Bi4O5Br2.

The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of the CQDs, Bi4O5Br2 and CQDs/Bi4O5Br2 series samples are shown in Fig. 3(a) and (c). As shown in Fig. 3(a), CQDs have a vast absorption range with an initial absorption wavelength of approximately 1400 nm and have good absorption of ultraviolet, visible and even near-infrared light. They exhibited strong PL when exposed to UV light because the original yellowish CQDs solution emitted green light when exposed to UV light (inset in Fig. 3(a)). Nevertheless, CQDs also show excellent up-conversion PL performance. As shown in Fig. 3(b), when the excitation wavelengths are 600, 700 and 800 nm, the CQDs show sharp emission peaks at 398, 465 and 531 nm, respectively. It indicates that adding CQDs can transform long-wavelength infrared light into short-wavelength ultraviolet or visible photons through the up-conversion function, greatly expanding solar spectrum utilisation in the field of photocatalysis. The wide spectral absorption benefits from CQD’s narrow band gap are calculated as 0.64 eV using the classical Tauc method (Fig. S2(a)), as reported in literature [38]. As shown in Fig. 3(c), Bi4O5Br2 is a typical wide band gap semiconductor with an upper limit of absorption wavelength of approximately 450 nm. After CQDs are dotted, the absorption limit of the CQDs/Bi4O5Br2 composite is significantly red-shifted. As a result, the sample colour is gradually deepened to red, indicating that the absorbance range is enhanced. However, when too many CQDs are introduced, the overload causes the absorbance wavelength to drop again.

As shown in Fig. S2(a) and (b), the band gaps of pure Bi4O5Br2 and CBiOBr4 obtained by the Tauc method are 2.54 and 2.38 eV, respectively. The band gap reduction of the composite sample may be due to the shift of the Bi4O5Br2 VB (CB) edge caused by electronic interface coupling. The VB positions of CQDs and Bi4O5Br2 are determined by VB XPS spectra (Fig. 3(d) and (e)) and are located at − 0.69 and 1.68 eV, respectively [44]. On the basis of the two components’ band gap and VB edge data, the CB edge positions of CQDs and Bi4O5Br2 are − 1.33 and − 0.86 eV according to the formula:

To further verify the accuracy of the energy level measurement, the Mott–Schottky (Fig. S3) test was conducted on Bi4O5Br2, and the measured flat band potential was − 0.8 eV. Because Bi4O5Br2 is an n-type semiconductor, it is inferred that the VB edge of Bi4O5Br2 is near − 0.8 eV. This is consistent with the above XPS VB spectrum measurement. Furthermore, from the energy level diagram (Fig. S4), the VB of CQDs is close to the CB of Bi4O5Br2, which is favourable for recombining holes in the valence band of CQDs and electrons in the conduction band of Bi4O5Br2. Hence, it provides favourable conditions to form Z-scheme heterojunctions.

Clarifying the charge transfer path is not only an important basis for analysing carrier dynamics but also a vital means of understanding the photocatalysis mechanism. To disclose the charge transfer path, we performed detailed XPS analysis ( Fig. 4) and UPS tests (Fig. S5) on CQDs, Bi4O5Br2 and CBiOBr4. The C 1 s spectrum in Fig. 4(a) consists of two signals with binding energies of 287.1 and 284.6 eV in CQDs, assigned to sp2-bonded C (N–CN) and sp3 hybridised C–C bonds of the fortuitous carbon used for calibration [34]. The O 1 s spectra of CQDs, Bi4O5Br2 and CBiOBr4 are shown in Fig. 4(b) and (d). The O 1 s signals of 530.4 and 532.1 eV in CQDs are attributed to the CO and C–O bonds [45]. At 528.9 and 530.2 eV, the O 1 s signal in Bi4O5Br2 corresponds to lattice O and adsorbed oxygen species [13]. The large difference in O 1 s binding energy between the two materials means that combining the two is conducive to forming a built-in electric field, which is beneficial to charge transfer. By comparing the spectra of O 1 s from Bi4O5Br2 and CBiOBr4, adding CQDs results in a significant negative shift of binding energy of approximately 0.1 eV (Fig. 4(d)). The same negative shift occurs when the 3d binding energy of Br is observed (Fig. 4(c)). The 3d binding energy of the composite sample decreased by approximately 0.2 eV compared with that of pure Bi4O5Br2 [14]. The VB of Bi4O5Br2 mainly comprises O 1 s and Br 4p orbitals. Reduction in binding energy means that the negative shift of the VB of the CQDs/Bi4O5Br2 heterojunction is more conducive to the formation of
• O
2
−
and construction of the Z-type heterojunction [12].

The work function (Φ) is a vital parameter to reveal electron transfer in heterostructured semiconductor materials. To further analyse the charge transfer mechanism, UPS tests were performed on CQDs, Bi4O5Br2 and CBiOBr4 (Fig. S5) to obtain secondary electron cut-off edges, which were 17.30, 14.68 and 16.15 eV, respectively. The corresponding work function Wf is calculated as 3.92, 6.54 and 5.07 eV, according to the following formula [46]:
(3)
W
f
= h v − S E C ( h v = 21.22 eV )

The work function is the energy difference between the material’s Fermi and vacuum energy levels. The larger the work function is, the lower is the Fermi level. Therefore, the Fermi level of Bi4O5Br2 is lower than that of CQDs, and the Fermi level of the CBiOBr4 heterojunction is between two monomers. Therefore, when CQDs are in close contact with Bi4O5Br2, electrons enter Bi4O5Br2 from CQDs to achieve the same Fermi energy level in equilibrium. As a result, an internal electric field is generated at the interface between Bi4O5Br2 and CQDs. Therefore, the UPS test effectively proves the direct Z-scheme path of charge transfer in the CQDs/Bi4O5Br2 heterojunction [34].

To explore the interfacial charge transfer resistance of the Bi4O5Br2 monomer and CQDs/Bi4O5Br2 materials, as shown in Fig. 5(a), the EIS technique was used for measurement and analysis. The magnitude of the charge transfer resistance at the electrode surface is evaluated with the semicircular arc diameter in the Nyquist plot, where a small diameter implies a low resistance. As the percentage of CQDs composite increases, the Nyquist plot diameter of the composite decreases gradually, indicating that the material’s resistance decreases slowly [47]. CQDs have a special conjugated π structure that allows them to act as effective electron transporters, accelerating the interfacial charge transfer. However, when the content of CQDs is too high, a charge overload phenomenon occurs, which affects the charge transfer, with CBiOBr4 having the lowest resistance.

Fig. 5(b) and (c) shows the transient photocurrent response of the photocatalysts. Under xenon lamp irradiation (Fig. 5(b)), all CQDs/Bi4O5Br2 materials show higher photocurrent density than pure Bi4O5Br2, with the photocurrent of CBiOBr4 being 9.56 times that of pure Bi4O5Br2. In CQDs/Bi4O5Br2 composite samples, the photocurrent density of CBiOBr4 is as high as 1.2 μA/cm2. This may be because fewer CQDs cannot effectively separate carriers and excessive CQDs may affect the light absorption of Bi4O5Br2, reducing the productivity of photogenerated electrons. When exposed to visible light with a wavelength greater than 400 nm, CBiOBr4 exhibited a good photocurrent response (Fig. 5(c)) whereas pure Bi4O5Br2 exhibited only approximately 0.01 μA/cm2 [48].

Therefore, we further performed PL tests on CQDs and CQDs/Bi4O5Br2 composites (Fig. 5(d)) to investigate the charge separation efficiency of the samples. Under 300 nm excitation, the PL emission peaks of all samples were approximately 420 nm. Furthermore, the emission peak of pure Bi4O5Br2 was the strongest, and that of CBiOBr4 was the weakest, indicating that it has a low carrier recombination rate and high quantum yield. The outcome provides evidence for the effective degradation of BPA and other pollutants. This phenomenon can be attributed to the efficient electron transfer from Bi4O5Br2 to CQDs with a conjugated π structure, which leads to the spatial separation of electrons and holes [49].

Excessive human exposure to BPA leads to heart disease, induces diabetes and liver disease, affects the brain development of fetuses and children, and even increases the risk of prostate and breast cancer. Therefore, it is highly harmful to the human body. In this experiment, the photocatalytic activities of Bi4O5Br2 and CQDs/Bi4O5Br2 composites were performed under xenon light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 6(a), CQDs/Bi4O5Br2 exhibits better photocatalytic performance than pure Bi4O5Br2 under direct xenon lamp irradiation. CBiOBr4 has the best performance, and its BPA degradation rate reaches 96% within 60 min, whereas the degradation rate of Bi4O5Br2 alone is only 30%. Fig. S7 shows the change in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CBiOBr4 degrading BPA under visible light, where the peak at 276 nm corresponds to the residual BPA. We can see that the degradation of BPA is fully completed. However, it does not mean that the compound is completely converted to CO2 and H2O. Therefore, TOC analysis was performed on the CBiOBr4 photocatalyst to obtain the mineralisation degree (Fig. S8). The total amount of organic matter in the BPA degradation solution decreased as the irradiation time increased. The TOC concentration dropped sharply when the maximum BPA removal rate was reached, implying that degradation to low molecular weight products and mineralisation co-occur [50]. After 100 min of degradation, the TOC removal rate reached 37% and increased continuously. This indicates that the CBiOBr4 photocatalyst can efficiently degrade BPA and can form other more favourable substances for degradation. In sharp contrast, by adding a 400 nm filter to sift ultraviolet components during xenon lamp irradiation, the BPA degradation efficiency of the Bi4O5Br2 monomer within 100 min is only 1.44% ( Fig. 7). However, CBiOBr4 can still degrade 100% BPA at the same time interval. The degradation efficiency is increased by 69 times, and the key reason is that the up-conversion performance of CQDs and Z-scheme charge transfer play key roles. To test the versatility of the current catalyst on degradation targets, we also performed degradation experiments on tetracycline (TC) under a xenon lamp. As shown in Fig. 6(b), TC degradation by CBiOBr4 reached 97% in 18 min whereas the degradation of Bi4O5Br2 monomer was only 37%. Table S2 lists the comparative studies of different active photocatalysts for BPA and TC degradation based on bismuth oxide semiconductor catalysts. The key indicators of catalyst dosage, degradation time and degradation rate are used to judge catalytic performance quality. Under similar catalyst dosage, the currently synthesised CQDs modified with Bi4O5Br2 composites have excellent photocatalytic performance and are better than most reported photocatalysts.

For deeper understanding of the photocatalytic degradation process, the reaction rate constant K app is obtained through the kinetic fitting of the reaction degradation curve using the following formula:
(4)
ln
(
C
t
C
0
)
= −
K
a p p
t
where C 0 and C t are the initial and intermediate concentrations at the initial moment and time t of the dye, respectively [45]. As shown in Fig. S9, the linear relationship between ln (C 0/C t) and t shows that the photocatalytic reaction is a pseudo-first-order. CBiOBr4 has the highest reaction rate, and the K app values of BPA and TC degradation are 0.04782 and 0.230 min−1, respectively, with both being approximately 10 times the corresponding K app value when pure Bi4O5Br2 degrades the two substances. The CBiOBr4 sample with the highest photodegradability was used as a model photocatalyst, and the photocatalyst’s stability was evaluated by cycling experiments. The recycling test is performed four times for BPA removal. As shown in Fig. 6(d), the degradation efficiency remained stable after four cycles of BPA degradation. The XRD test of the CBiOBr4 photocatalyst after four cycles (Fig. S10) shows that the sample structure has no noticeable change before and after the cycle. This indicates that the present CBiOBr4 material has high recyclability and is suitable for broad application in industrial production and life. The material achieved excellent photocatalytic performance while maintaining a low adsorption rate. As shown in Fig. S11, the adsorption–degradation plots show that CBiOBr4 has lower adsorption and higher degradation performances, which is one of the reasons for its high cycling stability.

The DMPO-

signals of Bi4O5Br2 and CBiOBr4 can be observed under xenon lamp (300 W) irradiation. As shown in Fig. 8(a), the EPR signal of DMPO-

of CBiOBr4 is stronger than that of Bi4O5Br2, indicating that CBiOBr4 produces a higher concentration of

By performing radical capture experiments on the tetracycline solvent under a xenon lamp (Fig. 8(d)), adding K2Cr2O7 (e− scavenger) and isopropanol (IPA, •OH scavenger) did not have much effect on the TC degradation rate. The rate decreased slightly after adding ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA, h+ scavenger). However, after adding benzoquinone (BQ,
● O
2
−
scavenger), there was almost no degradation in the tetracycline solvent. The behaviour is nearly consistent with the test results of DMPO-•OH and DMPO-
● O
2
−
.

On the basis of the above measurements and analysis, we finally discuss the possible mechanism of BPA degradation using the CBiOBr4 photocatalyst under two light conditions. As shown in Fig. 9(a), when a Xe lamp is directly illuminated, photons with energy greater than the band gap of Bi4O5Br2 (>2.54 eV) in incident light can excite electrons on the Bi4O5Br2 VB to its CB. The e− on the Bi4O5Br2 CB directly combines with h+ on the CQDs VB at the interface when affected by the internal electric field generated at the contact interface between the CQDs and Bi4O5Br2. The contact follows a direct Z-scheme heterostructure [52]. Therefore, even if the radiation wavelength exceeds 400 nm, Z-type charge transfer can still be maintained (Fig. 9(b)). However, the electron excitation mechanism differs from direct xenon lamp irradiation. Only a small number of electrons are transferred to the CB of Bi4O5Br2 through direct excitation when a 400 nm filter is used to sift ultraviolet light. However, owing to the up-conversion PL properties of CQDs (Fig. 3(b)), they can simultaneously absorb two or more long-wavelength photons to produce up-conversion fluorescence with a shorter wavelength than the excitation. In addition, the composite catalyst transfers electrons for redox reactions via a direct Z-scheme photocatalytic mechanism. In this regard, CQDs/Bi4O5Br2 composites effectively broaden the use of visible and infrared light, improve light utilisation efficiency and have excellent carrier concentration and charge separation efficiency, which significantly improves BPA degradation.

We successfully introduced CQDs to the surface of Bi4O5Br2 using a simple hydrothermal method to form a Z-scheme heterojunction of CQDs/Bi4O5Br2. The good matching between the band structures of CQDs and Bi4O5Br2 is conducive to separating photogenerated carriers effectively. Furthermore, the up-conversion PL characteristics of CQDs further broaden the use of visible light and even infrared light so that the VB electrons of Bi4O5Br2 can be effectively excited under visible light. Therefore, efficient photodegradation of BPA can be achieved under visible light irradiation. The design of an energy band that matches semiconductor heterojunction photocatalysts in this work can provide a new idea for constructing semiconductor heterojunctions. Hence, the unique energy band structure and up-conversion performance of CQDs can be enhanced to degrade pollutants efficiently.
